records.ety: [Probably of Celtic origin; cf. W. rhwbiaw, gael. rub.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Rubbed (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Rubbing.]

defs: To subject (a body) to the action of something moving over its surface with pressure and friction, especially to the action of something moving back and forth; as, to rub the flesh with the hand; to rub wood with sandpaper.
quotes: It shall be expedient, after that body is cleaned, to rub the body with a coarse linen cloth.

defs: To move over the surface of (a body) with pressure and friction; to graze; to chafe; as, the boat rubs the ground.
To cause (a body) to move with pressure and friction along a surface; as, to rub the hand over the body.
quotes: Two bones rubbed hard against one another.

defs: To spread a substance thinly over; to smear.
quotes: The smoothed plank, . . . New rubbed with balm.

defs: To scour; to burnish; to polish; to brighten; to cleanse; -- often with up or over; as, to rub up silver.
quotes: The whole business of our redemption is to rub over the defaced copy of the creation.

defs: To hinder; to cross; to thwart.
quotes: 'T is the duke's pleasure, Whose disposition, all the world well knows, Will not be rubbed nor stopped.

defs: To move along the surface of a body with pressure; to grate; as, a wheel rubs against the gatepost.
To fret; to chafe; as, to rub upon a sore.
To move or pass with difficulty; as, to rub through woods, as huntsmen; to rub through the world.
That which rubs; that which tends to hinder or obstruct motion or progress; hindrance; obstruction, an impediment; especially, a difficulty or obstruction hard to overcome; a pinch.
quotes: Every rub is smoothed on our way.
To sleep, perchance to dream; ay, there's the rub.
Upon this rub, the English ambassadors thought fit to demur.
One knows not, certainly, what other rubs might have been ordained for us by a wise Providence.

defs: Inequality of surface, as of the ground in the game of bowls; unevenness.
Something grating to the feelings; sarcasm; joke; as, a hard rub.

quotes: Flight shall leave no Greek a rub.

defs: A stone, commonly flat, used to sharpen cutting tools; a whetstone; -- called also rubstone.
